title: Trafalgar Square

infobox.value: [City of Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster"), London, England

([/trəˈfælɡər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Vealhurl-Trafalgar.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Vealhurl-Trafalgar.wav") [*trə-FAL-gər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [public square](/wiki/Public_square "Public square") in the [City of Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster") in [Central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London"). It was established in the early 19th century around the area known as [Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross "Charing Cross"). Its name commemorates the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"), the [British naval](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") victory in the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") over [France](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") and [Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700-1808) "History of Spain (1700-1808)") that took place on 21 October 1805 off the coast of [Cape Trafalgar](/wiki/Cape_Trafalgar "Cape Trafalgar").

**Trafalgar Square** ([/trəˈfælɡər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Vealhurl-Trafalgar.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Vealhurl-Trafalgar.wav") [*trə-FAL-gər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [public square](/wiki/Public_square "Public square") in the [City of Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster") in [Central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London"). It was established in the early 19th century around the area known as [Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross "Charing Cross"). Its name commemorates the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"), the [British naval](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") victory in the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") over [France](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") and [Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700-1808) "History of Spain (1700-1808)") that took place on 21 October 1805 off the coast of [Cape Trafalgar](/wiki/Cape_Trafalgar "Cape Trafalgar").

The area around Trafalgar Square has been a significant landmark since the 1200s, as distances from London are measured from Charing Cross.[[1]](#cite_note-charingcross_feature3-1) The [King's Mews](/wiki/Royal_Mews "Royal Mews") occupied part of the site until the reign of [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV"), who moved them to [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"), allowing the area to be redeveloped by [John Nash](/wiki/John_Nash_(architect) "John Nash (architect)"). Progress stalled after the death of Nash in 1835, and the square did not open until 1844. It is faced by buildings including the [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery"), [St Martin-in-the-Fields](/wiki/St_Martin-in-the-Fields "St Martin-in-the-Fields"), [Canada House](/wiki/Canada_House "Canada House") and [South Africa House](/wiki/South_Africa_House "South Africa House"). The square also contains several pieces of public sculpture, including the 169-foot (52 m) [Nelson's Column](/wiki/Nelson%27s_Column "Nelson's Column") in the centre, which commemorates Vice-Admiral [Horatio Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson")'s victory at Trafalgar; an [equestrian statue of Charles I](/wiki/Equestrian_statue_of_Charles_I,_Charing_Cross "Equestrian statue of Charles I, Charing Cross"); and the [fourth plinth](/wiki/Fourth_plinth "Fourth plinth"), which since 1999 has hosted a variety of artworks.

The square has been used for community gatherings and political [demonstrations](/wiki/Demonstration_(people) "Demonstration (people)"), including [Bloody Sunday](/wiki/Bloody_Sunday_(1887) "Bloody Sunday (1887)") in 1887, the culmination of the first [Aldermaston March](/wiki/Aldermaston_March "Aldermaston March"), [anti-war protests](/wiki/Anti-war_protests "Anti-war protests") and campaigns against [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"). A [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree") has been donated to the square by the [government of Norway](/wiki/Government_of_Norway "Government of Norway") since 1947 and is erected for twelve days before and after Christmas Day. The square is a centre of annual celebrations on [New Year's Eve](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Eve "New Year's Eve") and was known for its [feral pigeons](/wiki/Feral_pigeon "Feral pigeon") until their removal in the early 21st century.

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

The square is named after the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"), a [British naval](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") victory in the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") with France and Spain that took place on 21 October 1805 off the coast of [Cape Trafalgar](/wiki/Cape_Trafalgar "Cape Trafalgar"), southwest Spain, although it was not named as such until 1835.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008934-2)

The name "Trafalgar" is a [Spanish word](/wiki/Influence_of_Arabic_on_Spanish "Influence of Arabic on Spanish") of [Arabic origin](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Arabic_origin "List of English words of Arabic origin"), derived from either *Taraf al-Ghar* (طرف الغار 'cape of the cave/laurel')[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-burton-5) or *Taraf al-Gharb* (طرف الغرب 'extremity of the west').[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[5]](#cite_note-burton-5)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Trafalgar Square is owned by the King in Right of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown")[[a]](#cite_note-8) and managed by the [Greater London Authority](/wiki/Greater_London_Authority "Greater London Authority"), while [Westminster City Council](/wiki/Westminster_City_Council "Westminster City Council") owns the roads around the square, including the pedestrianised area of the North Terrace.[[8]](#cite_note-9) The square contains a large central area with roadways on three sides and a terrace to the north, in front of the [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery"). The roads around the square form part of the [A4](/wiki/A4_road_(England) "A4 road (England)"), a major road running west of the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London").[[9]](#cite_note-gmap-10) Originally, traffic travelled both directions around the square until a one-way clockwise gyratory system was introduced on 26 April 1926.[[10]](#cite_note-11) In 2003, the northern side was closed and the road width reduced.[[11]](#cite_note-nytimes.com.2003-12)

[Nelson's Column](/wiki/Nelson%27s_Column "Nelson's Column") is in the centre of the square, flanked by fountains designed by the English architect Sir [Edwin Lutyens](/wiki/Edwin_Lutyens "Edwin Lutyens") between 1937 and 1939[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker200543-13) (replacements for two of [Peterhead granite](/wiki/Boddam,_Aberdeenshire#Peterhead_granite "Boddam, Aberdeenshire"), now in Canada) and guarded by four monumental bronze lions sculpted by Sir [Edwin Landseer](/wiki/Edwin_Landseer "Edwin Landseer").[[13]](#cite_note-thornbury-14) At the top of the column is a statue of [Horatio Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson"), who commanded the British Navy at the Battle of Trafalgar.

Surrounding the square are the National Gallery on the north side and [St Martin-in-the-Fields](/wiki/St_Martin-in-the-Fields "St Martin-in-the-Fields") Church to the east.[[13]](#cite_note-thornbury-14) Also on the east is [South Africa House](/wiki/South_Africa_House "South Africa House"), and facing it across the square is [Canada House](/wiki/Canada_House "Canada House"). To the south-west is [The Mall](/wiki/The_Mall,_London "The Mall, London"), which leads towards [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") via [Admiralty Arch](/wiki/Admiralty_Arch "Admiralty Arch"), while [Whitehall](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall") is to the south and the [Strand](/wiki/Strand,_London "Strand, London") to the east. [Charing Cross Road](/wiki/Charing_Cross_Road "Charing Cross Road") passes between the National Gallery and the church.[[9]](#cite_note-gmap-10)

[London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground")'s [Charing Cross station](/wiki/Charing_Cross_tube_station "Charing Cross tube station") on the [Northern](/wiki/Northern_line "Northern line") and [Bakerloo](/wiki/Bakerloo_line "Bakerloo line") lines has an exit in the square. The lines had separate stations; the Bakerloo line branch was called Trafalgar Square until the two were linked in 1979 during the construction of the [Jubilee line](/wiki/Jubilee_line "Jubilee line")[[14]](#cite_note-15) (which was rerouted to [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster_tube_station "Westminster tube station") in 1999).[[15]](#cite_note-16) Other nearby tube stations are [Embankment](/wiki/Embankment_tube_station "Embankment tube station") connecting the [District](/wiki/District_line "District line"), [Circle](/wiki/Circle_line_(London_Underground) "Circle line (London Underground)"), Northern and Bakerloo lines, and [Leicester Square](/wiki/Leicester_Square_tube_station "Leicester Square tube station") on the Northern and [Piccadilly](/wiki/Piccadilly_line "Piccadilly line") lines.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

Several bus routes run through the square, including routes [3](/wiki/London_Buses_route_3 "London Buses route 3"), [6](/wiki/London_Buses_route_6 "London Buses route 6"), [9](/wiki/London_Buses_route_9 "London Buses route 9"), [11](/wiki/London_Buses_route_11 "London Buses route 11"), [12](/wiki/London_Buses_route_12 "London Buses route 12"), [13](/wiki/London_Buses_route_13 "London Buses route 13"), [15](/wiki/London_Buses_route_15 "London Buses route 15"), [23](/wiki/London_Buses_route_23 "London Buses route 23"), [24](/wiki/London_Buses_route_24 "London Buses route 24"), [29](/wiki/London_Buses_route_29 "London Buses route 29"), [53](/wiki/London_Buses_route_53 "London Buses route 53"), [87](/wiki/London_Buses_route_87 "London Buses route 87"), [88](/wiki/London_Buses_route_88 "London Buses route 88"), [91](/wiki/London_Buses_route_91 "London Buses route 91"), [139](/wiki/London_Buses_route_139 "London Buses route 139"), [159](/wiki/London_Buses_route_159 "London Buses route 159"), [176](/wiki/London_Buses_route_176 "London Buses route 176"), [453](/wiki/London_Buses_route_453 "London Buses route 453").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

A point in Trafalgar Square is regarded as the official centre of London in legislation and when [measuring distances from the capital](/wiki/Kilometre_zero#Great_Britain "Kilometre zero").[[18]](#cite_note-charingcross_feature-19)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

[![A painting by James Pollard showing the square](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Trafalgar_Square_by_James_Pollard.jpg/250px-Trafalgar_Square_by_James_Pollard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_Square_by_James_Pollard.jpg)

A painting by [James Pollard](/wiki/James_Pollard "James Pollard") showing the square before the erection of Nelson's Column

Building work on the south side of the square in the late 1950s revealed [river terrace](/wiki/River_terrace "River terrace") deposits containing fossils from the [Last Interglacial](/wiki/Last_Interglacial "Last Interglacial"), around 130-115,000 years ago. Among the findings were the remains of [cave lions](/wiki/Panthera_spelaea "Panthera spelaea"), rhinoceroses, [straight-tusked elephant](/wiki/Straight-tusked_elephant "Straight-tusked elephant") and [hippopotamus](/wiki/Hippopotamus "Hippopotamus"), as well as feces of [cave hyenas](/wiki/Cave_hyena "Cave hyena").[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The site has been significant since the 13th century. During the reign of [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") it hosted the [King's Mews](/wiki/King%27s_Mews "King's Mews"), running north from the T-junction in the south, [Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross "Charing Cross"), where the [Strand](/wiki/Strand,_London "Strand, London") from the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") meets [Whitehall](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall") coming north from [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster "Westminster").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008934-2) From the reign of [Richard II](/wiki/Richard_II_of_England "Richard II of England") to that of [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England"), the mews was at the western end of the Strand. The name "Royal Mews" comes from the practice of keeping [hawks](/wiki/Hawk "Hawk") here for [moulting](/wiki/Moulting "Moulting"); "mew" is an old word for this. After a fire in 1534, the mews were rebuilt as stables, and remained here until [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") moved them to [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace").[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Clearance and development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Clearance and development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Perspective_View_of_the_Royal_Stables_at_Charing_Cross_-_J._Maurer%2C_1747_-_grosvenor11544.jpg/250px-Perspective_View_of_the_Royal_Stables_at_Charing_Cross_-_J._Maurer%2C_1747_-_grosvenor11544.jpg)](/wiki/File:Perspective_View_of_the_Royal_Stables_at_Charing_Cross_-_J._Maurer,_1747_-_grosvenor11544.jpg)

Kent's Royal Stables, with the Great Mews in the foreground, in 1747. (To the right is St Martin-in-the-Fields.)

After 1732 the King's Mews were divided into the Great Mews and the smaller Green Mews to the north by the Crown Stables, a large block, built to the designs of [William Kent](/wiki/William_Kent "William Kent"). Its site is occupied by the National Gallery.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMace197629-24)

In 1826 the [Commissioners of Woods, Forests and Land Revenues](/wiki/Commissioners_of_Woods,_Forests_and_Land_Revenues "Commissioners of Woods, Forests and Land Revenues") instructed [John Nash](/wiki/John_Nash_(architect) "John Nash (architect)") to draw up plans for clearing a large area south of Kent's stable block, and as far east as [St Martin's Lane](/wiki/St_Martin%27s_Lane "St Martin's Lane"). His plans left open the whole area of what became Trafalgar Square, except for a block in the centre, which he reserved for a new building for the [Royal Academy of Arts](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Arts "Royal Academy of Arts").[[24]](#cite_note-survey-25) The plans included the demolition and redevelopment of buildings between St Martin's Lane and the Strand and the construction of a road (now called Duncannon Street) across the [churchyard](/wiki/Churchyard "Churchyard") of [St Martin-in-the-Fields](/wiki/St_Martin-in-the-Fields "St Martin-in-the-Fields").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMace197637-26) The Charing Cross Act was passed in 1826 and clearance started soon after.[[24]](#cite_note-survey-25) Nash died soon after construction started, impeding its progress. The square was to be named after [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") commemorating his accession to the throne in 1830.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2003176-27) Around 1835 it was decided that the square would be named after the Battle of Trafalgar as suggested by the architect [George Ledwell Taylor](/wiki/George_Ledwell_Taylor "George Ledwell Taylor"), commemorating Nelson's victory over the French and Spanish in 1805 during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008934-2)[[27]](#cite_note-28)

[![Animated picture using ten frames taken by Wordsworth Donisthorpe](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Trafalgar_Square_1890_-_ten_remaining_frames_by_Wordsworth_Donisthorpe.gif/250px-Trafalgar_Square_1890_-_ten_remaining_frames_by_Wordsworth_Donisthorpe.gif)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_Square_1890_-_ten_remaining_frames_by_Wordsworth_Donisthorpe.gif)

Ten frames of Trafalgar Square shot by [Wordsworth Donisthorpe](/wiki/Wordsworth_Donisthorpe "Wordsworth Donisthorpe") in 1890

After the clearance development progressed slowly. The National Gallery was built on the north side between 1832 and 1838 to a design by [William Wilkins](/wiki/William_Wilkins_(architect) "William Wilkins (architect)"),[[24]](#cite_note-survey-25) and in 1837 the Treasury approved Wilkins' plan for the laying out of the square, but it was not put into effect.[[28]](#cite_note-am-29) In April 1840, following Wilkins' death, new plans by [Charles Barry](/wiki/Charles_Barry "Charles Barry") were accepted, and construction started within weeks.[[24]](#cite_note-survey-25)[[29]](#cite_note-report-30) For Barry, as for Wilkins, a major consideration was increasing the visual impact of the National Gallery, which had been widely criticised for its lack of grandeur. He dealt with the complex sloping site by excavating the main area to the level of the footway between [Cockspur Street](/wiki/Cockspur_Street "Cockspur Street") and the Strand,[[30]](#cite_note-ce-31) and constructing a 15-foot (4.6 m) high balustraded terrace with a roadway on the north side, and steps at each end leading to the main level.[[29]](#cite_note-report-30) Wilkins had proposed a similar solution with a central flight of steps.[[28]](#cite_note-am-29) All the stonework was of [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") from [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen").[[29]](#cite_note-report-30) In 1845 four [Bude-Lights](/wiki/Bude-Light "Bude-Light") with octagonal glass lanterns were installed. Two, opposite the National Gallery, are on tall bronze columns, and two, in the south-west and south-east corners of the square, on shorter bronze columns on top of wider granite columns. They were designed by Barry and manufactured by Stevens and Son, of [Southwark](/wiki/Southwark "Southwark").[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In 1841 it was decided that two fountains should be included in the layout.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMace1976107-33) The estimated budget, excluding paving and sculptures, was £11,000.[[29]](#cite_note-report-30) The earth removed was used to level [Green Park](/wiki/Green_Park "Green Park").[[30]](#cite_note-ce-31) The square was originally surfaced with [tarmacadam](/wiki/Tarmacadam "Tarmacadam"), which was replaced with stone in the 1920s.[[33]](#cite_note-westminster-34)

Trafalgar Square was opened to the public on 1 May 1844.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

### Nelson's Column

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Nelson's Column")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/London%2C_Trafalgar_Square%2C_Nelson%27s_Column_--_2016_--_4851.jpg/250px-London%2C_Trafalgar_Square%2C_Nelson%27s_Column_--_2016_--_4851.jpg)](/wiki/File:London,_Trafalgar_Square,_Nelson%27s_Column_--_2016_--_4851.jpg)

[Nelson's Column](/wiki/Nelson%27s_Column "Nelson's Column")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/TrafalgarSquareLion.JPG/250px-TrafalgarSquareLion.JPG)](/wiki/File:TrafalgarSquareLion.JPG)

The lions at [Nelson's Column](/wiki/Nelson%27s_Column "Nelson's Column") were not finished until nearly 30 years after the square opened.

Main article: [Nelson's Column](/wiki/Nelson%27s_Column "Nelson's Column")

[Nelson's Column](/wiki/Nelson%27s_Column "Nelson's Column") was planned independently of Barry's work. In 1838 a Nelson Memorial Committee had approached [Her Majesty's Government](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Government "Her Majesty's Government") proposing that a monument to the victory of Trafalgar, funded by public subscription, should be erected in the square. A competition was held and won by the architect [William Railton](/wiki/William_Railton "William Railton"), who proposed a 218-foot-3-inch (66.52 m) [Corinthinan](/wiki/Corinthian_order "Corinthian order") column topped by a statue of Nelson and guarded by four sculpted lions. The design was approved, but received widespread objections from the public. Construction went ahead beginning in 1840 but with the height reduced to 145 feet 3 inches (44.27 m).[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2003177-36) The column was completed and the statue raised in November 1843.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMace197690-37)

The last of the bronze reliefs on the column's pedestals was not completed until May 1854, and the four lions, although part of the original design, were only added in 1867.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMace1976107–108-38) Each lion weighs seven tons.[[38]](#cite_note-39) A hoarding remained around the base of Nelson's Column for some years and some of its upper scaffolding remained in place.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Landseer, the sculptor, had asked for a lion that had died at the [London Zoo](/wiki/London_Zoo "London Zoo") to be brought to his studio. He took so long to complete sketches that its corpse began to [decompose](/wiki/Decomposition "Decomposition") and some parts had to be improvised. The statues have paws that resemble cats more than lions.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Barry was unhappy about Nelson's Column being placed in the square. In July 1840, when its foundations had been laid, he told a parliamentary select committee that "it would in my opinion be desirable that the area should be wholly free from all insulated objects of art".[[29]](#cite_note-report-30)

In 1940, during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the *[Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel")* of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") developed secret plans to transfer Nelson's Column to [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin")[[b]](#cite_note-43) after an expected [German invasion](/wiki/Operation_Sea_Lion "Operation Sea Lion"), as related by the historian [Norman Longmate](/wiki/Norman_Longmate "Norman Longmate") in *If Britain Had Fallen* (1972).[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongmate2012137-42)

The square has been [Grade I listed](/wiki/Grade_I_listed "Grade I listed") on the [Register of Historic Parks and Gardens](/wiki/Register_of_Historic_Parks_and_Gardens_of_special_historic_interest_in_England "Register of Historic Parks and Gardens of special historic interest in England") since 1996.[[42]](#cite_note-NHLEGarden-44)

### Terrorist bombings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Terrorist bombings")]

See also: [Suffragette bombing and arson campaign](/wiki/Suffragette_bombing_and_arson_campaign "Suffragette bombing and arson campaign")

The square was the target of two [suffragette](/wiki/Suffragette "Suffragette") bombings in 1913 and 1914. This was as part of the [suffragette bombing and arson campaign](/wiki/Suffragette_bombing_and_arson_campaign "Suffragette bombing and arson campaign") of 1912–1914, in which suffragettes carried out a series of politically-motivated bombing and arson attacks nationwide as part of their campaign for [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage").[[43]](#cite_note-BL-45)

The first attack occurred on 15 May 1913. A bomb was planted in the public area outside the National Gallery, but failed to explode.[[44]](#cite_note-46) A second attack occurred at St Martin-in-the-Fields church at the north-east corner of the square on 4 April 1914. A bomb exploded inside the church, blowing out the windows and showering passers-by with broken glass. The bomb then started a fire.[[45]](#cite_note-Webb-47)[[46]](#cite_note-48) In the aftermath a mass of people rushed to the scene, many of whom aggressively expressed their anger towards the suffragettes.[[45]](#cite_note-Webb-47) Churches were a particular target during the campaign, as it was believed that the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") was complicit in reinforcing opposition to women's suffrage.[[47]](#cite_note-49) Between 1913 and 1914 thirty-two churches were attacked nationwide.[[48]](#cite_note-50) In the weeks after the bombing, there were also attacks on [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") and [St Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Cathedral "St Paul's Cathedral").[[43]](#cite_note-BL-45)

### Redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Redevelopment")]

A major 18-month redevelopment of the square led by [W.S. Atkins](/wiki/Atkins_(company) "Atkins (company)") with [Foster and Partners](/wiki/Foster_and_Partners "Foster and Partners") as sub-consultants was completed in 2003. The work involved closing the eastbound road along the north side and diverting traffic around the other three sides of the square, demolishing the central section of the northern retaining wall and inserting a wide set of steps to the pedestrianised terrace in front of the National Gallery. The construction includes two lifts for disabled access, public toilets and a café. Access between the square and the gallery had been by two crossings at the northeast and northwest corners.[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008935-52)

## Statues and monuments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Statues and monuments")]

Main article: [List of public art in Trafalgar Square and the vicinity](/wiki/List_of_public_art_in_Trafalgar_Square_and_the_vicinity "List of public art in Trafalgar Square and the vicinity")

### Plinths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Plinths")]

[![Sir Henry Havelock's statue](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Statue_of_Henry_Havelock%2C_October_2014_%2815555081947%29.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Henry_Havelock%2C_October_2014_%2815555081947%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Henry_Havelock,_October_2014_(15555081947).jpg)

The [statue of Sir Henry Havelock](/wiki/Statue_of_Henry_Havelock,_Trafalgar_Square "Statue of Henry Havelock, Trafalgar Square") by [William Behnes](/wiki/William_Behnes "William Behnes")

Barry's scheme provided two plinths for sculptures on the north side of the square.[[51]](#cite_note-gn-53) A bronze [equestrian statue](/wiki/Equestrian_statue_of_George_IV,_Trafalgar_Square "Equestrian statue of George IV, Trafalgar Square") of George IV was designed by [Sir Francis Leggatt Chantrey](/wiki/Francis_Leggatt_Chantrey "Francis Leggatt Chantrey") and [Thomas Earle](/wiki/Thomas_Earle_(sculptor) "Thomas Earle (sculptor)"). It was originally intended to be placed atop the [Marble Arch](/wiki/Marble_Arch "Marble Arch"), but instead was installed on the eastern plinth in 1843, while the other plinths remained empty until late in the 20th century.[[52]](#cite_note-54)[[24]](#cite_note-survey-25)[[53]](#cite_note-55) There are two other statues on plinths, both installed during the 19th century: General [Sir Charles James Napier](/wiki/Charles_James_Napier "Charles James Napier") by [George Cannon Adams](/wiki/George_Cannon_Adams "George Cannon Adams") in the south-west corner in 1855, and Major-General [Sir Henry Havelock](/wiki/Henry_Havelock "Henry Havelock") by [William Behnes](/wiki/William_Behnes "William Behnes") in the south-east in 1861.[[24]](#cite_note-survey-25) In 2000 the [mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London"), [Ken Livingstone](/wiki/Ken_Livingstone "Ken Livingstone"), suggested replacing the statues with figures more familiar to the general public.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

#### Fourth plinth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Fourth plinth")]

Main article: [Fourth plinth](/wiki/Fourth_plinth "Fourth plinth")

In the 21st century the empty plinth in the north-west corner of the square, the "fourth plinth", has been used to show specially commissioned temporary artworks. The scheme was initiated by the [Royal Society of Arts](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Arts "Royal Society of Arts") and continued by the Fourth Plinth Commission, appointed by the mayor of London.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

### Other sculptures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other sculptures")]

There are three busts of admirals against the north wall of the square. Those of [John Jellicoe, 1st Earl Jellicoe](/wiki/John_Jellicoe,_1st_Earl_Jellicoe "John Jellicoe, 1st Earl Jellicoe") (by [Sir Charles Wheeler](/wiki/Charles_Wheeler_(sculptor) "Charles Wheeler (sculptor)")) and [David Beatty, 1st Earl Beatty](/wiki/David_Beatty,_1st_Earl_Beatty "David Beatty, 1st Earl Beatty") (by [William MacMillan](/wiki/William_McMillan_(sculptor) "William McMillan (sculptor)")) were installed in 1948 in conjunction with the square's fountains, which also commemorate them.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59) The third, of [First Sea Lord](/wiki/First_Sea_Lord "First Sea Lord") [Andrew Cunningham, 1st Viscount Cunningham of Hyndhope](/wiki/Andrew_Cunningham,_1st_Viscount_Cunningham_of_Hyndhope "Andrew Cunningham, 1st Viscount Cunningham of Hyndhope") (by the Czech sculptor [Franta Belsky](/wiki/Franta_Belsky "Franta Belsky")) was unveiled alongside them on 2 April 1967.[[58]](#cite_note-60)

On the south side of Trafalgar Square, on the site of the original Charing Cross, is a bronze [equestrian statue of Charles I](/wiki/Equestrian_statue_of_Charles_I,_Charing_Cross "Equestrian statue of Charles I, Charing Cross") by [Hubert Le Sueur](/wiki/Hubert_Le_Sueur "Hubert Le Sueur"). It was cast in 1633, and placed in its present position in 1678.[[59]](#cite_note-61)

The two statues on the lawn in front of the National Gallery are the [statue of James II](/wiki/Statue_of_James_II,_Trafalgar_Square "Statue of James II, Trafalgar Square") (designed by [Peter van Dievoet](/wiki/Peter_Van_Dievoet "Peter Van Dievoet")[[60]](#cite_note-62) and [Laurens van der Meulen](/wiki/Laurens_van_der_Meulen "Laurens van der Meulen") for the studio of [Grinling Gibbons](/wiki/Grinling_Gibbons "Grinling Gibbons"))[[61]](#cite_note-63) to the west of the portico, and of [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), a [replica of a work](/wiki/George_Washington_(Houdon) "George Washington (Houdon)") by [Jean-Antoine Houdon](/wiki/Jean-Antoine_Houdon "Jean-Antoine Houdon"), to the east.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008935-52) The latter was a gift from the government of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), installed in 1921.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008875-64)

Two statues erected in the 19th century have since been removed. One of [Edward Jenner](/wiki/Edward_Jenner "Edward Jenner"), pioneer of the [smallpox vaccine](/wiki/Smallpox_vaccine "Smallpox vaccine"), was set up in the south-west corner of the square in 1858, next to that of Napier. Sculpted by [William Calder Marshall](/wiki/William_Calder_Marshall "William Calder Marshall"), it showed Jenner sitting in a chair in a relaxed pose, and was inaugurated at a ceremony presided over by [Prince Albert](/wiki/Albert,_Prince_Consort "Albert, Prince Consort"). It was moved to [Kensington Gardens](/wiki/Kensington_Gardens "Kensington Gardens") in 1862.[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66) The other, of General [Charles George Gordon](/wiki/Charles_George_Gordon "Charles George Gordon") by [Hamo Thornycroft](/wiki/Hamo_Thornycroft "Hamo Thornycroft"), was erected on an 18-foot-high pedestal between the fountains in 1888. It was removed in 1943 and re-sited on the [Victoria Embankment](/wiki/Victoria_Embankment "Victoria Embankment") ten years later.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMace1976125–126-67)

## Fountains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Fountains")]

[![Fountain at Trafalgar Square, 2014](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Trafalgar_square_fountain%2C_June_7_2014.JPG/250px-Trafalgar_square_fountain%2C_June_7_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_square_fountain,_June_7_2014.JPG)

Fountain at Trafalgar Square, 2014

In 1841, following suggestions from the local paving board, Barry agreed that two fountains should be installed to counteract the effects of reflected heat and glare from the asphalt surface. The First Commissioner of Woods and Forests welcomed the plan because the fountains reduced the open space available for public gatherings and reduced the risk of riotous assembly.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMace197687-68) The fountains were fed from two wells, one in front of the National Gallery and one behind it connected by a tunnel. Water was pumped to the fountains by a steam engine housed in a building behind the gallery.[[24]](#cite_note-survey-25)

In the late-1930s it was decided to replace the pump and the centrepieces of the fountains. The new centrepieces, designed by Sir [Edwin Lutyens](/wiki/Edwin_Lutyens "Edwin Lutyens"), were memorials to Lord Jellicoe and Lord Beatty, although busts of the admirals, initially intended to be placed in the fountain surrounds were placed against the northern retaining wall when the project was completed after the Second World War.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMace1976130–1-69) The fountains cost almost £50,000. The original centrepieces were presented to the Canadian government and are now located in [Confederation Park](/wiki/Confederation_Park "Confederation Park") in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") and the [Wascana Centre](/wiki/Wascana_Centre "Wascana Centre") in [Regina, Saskatchewan](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan").[[68]](#cite_note-Guardian_20090529-70)[[69]](#cite_note-GardenFoundation-71)

A programme of restoration was completed by May 2009. The pump system was replaced with one capable of sending an 80-foot (24 m) jet of water into the air.[[70]](#cite_note-72) A [light-emitting diode](/wiki/Light-emitting_diode "Light-emitting diode") lighting system that can project different combinations of colours on to the fountains was installed to reduce the cost of lighting maintenance and to coincide with the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics").[[68]](#cite_note-Guardian_20090529-70)

## Pigeons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Pigeons")]

See also: [Save the Trafalgar Square Pigeons](/wiki/Save_the_Trafalgar_Square_Pigeons "Save the Trafalgar Square Pigeons")

[![Pigeons flocking to London's Trafalgar Square](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/People_feeding_pigeons_in_Trafalgar_Square_c.1993.jpg/250px-People_feeding_pigeons_in_Trafalgar_Square_c.1993.jpg)](/wiki/File:People_feeding_pigeons_in_Trafalgar_Square_c.1993.jpg)

People sitting on lions and feeding pigeons in the square

The square was once famous for [feral pigeons](/wiki/Feral_pigeon "Feral pigeon") and feeding them was a popular activity. Pigeons began flocking to the square before construction was completed and feed sellers became well known in the Victorian era.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2003181-73) The desirability of the birds' presence was contentious: their droppings disfigured the stonework and the flock, estimated at its peak to be 35,000, was considered a [health hazard](/wiki/Rock_dove#Human_health "Rock dove").[[72]](#cite_note-pidgeons-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75) A stall seller, Bernie Rayner, infamously sold bird seed to tourists at inflated prices.[[74]](#cite_note-ipidgeon-76)

In February 2001 the sale of bird seed in the square was stopped[[72]](#cite_note-pidgeons-74) and other measures were introduced to discourage the pigeons including the use of [birds of prey](/wiki/Falconry "Falconry").[[75]](#cite_note-77) Supporters continued to feed the birds, but in 2003 Livingstone enacted [bylaws](/wiki/Bylaw "Bylaw") to ban feeding them in the square.[[76]](#cite_note-78) In September 2007 [Westminster City Council](/wiki/Westminster_City_Council "Westminster City Council") passed further bylaws banning feeding birds on the [pedestrianised](/wiki/Pedestrianisation "Pedestrianisation") North Terrace and other pavements in the area.[[77]](#cite_note-79) Nelson's column was repaired from years of damage from pigeon droppings at a cost of £140,000.[[74]](#cite_note-ipidgeon-76)

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Events")]

### New Year

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: New Year")]

For many years, revellers celebrating the [New Year](/wiki/New_Year "New Year") have gathered in the square despite a lack of celebrations being arranged. The lack of official events was partly because the authorities were concerned that encouraging more partygoers would cause overcrowding. Since 2003 a fireworks display centred on the [London Eye](/wiki/London_Eye "London Eye") and [South Bank](/wiki/South_Bank "South Bank") of the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") has been provided as an alternative. Since 2014 New Year celebrations have been organised by the Greater London Authority in conjunction with [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"), who began ticketing the event to control crowd numbers.[[78]](#cite_note-80) The fireworks display was cancelled during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). An event scheduled to take place in the Square to welcome in 2022[[79]](#cite_note-81) was cancelled during the spread of the [SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Omicron_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant").[[80]](#cite_note-82)

### Christmas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Christmas")]

See also: [Trafalgar Square Christmas tree](/wiki/Trafalgar_Square_Christmas_tree "Trafalgar Square Christmas tree")

[![The Trafalgar Square Christmas tree](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Trafalgar_Square_Christmas_tree8.jpg/250px-Trafalgar_Square_Christmas_tree8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_Square_Christmas_tree8.jpg)

The Trafalgar Square Christmas tree in 2008

A [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") ceremony has been held in the square every year since 1947.[[81]](#cite_note-bbctree-83) A [Norway spruce](/wiki/Norway_spruce "Norway spruce") (or sometimes a [fir](/wiki/Fir "Fir")) is presented by Norway's [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city"), [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), as London's [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree"), a token of gratitude for Britain's support during the Second World War.[[81]](#cite_note-bbctree-83) ([Prince Olav](/wiki/Olav_V_of_Norway "Olav V of Norway") and the Norwegian government also lived in exile in London throughout the war.)[[81]](#cite_note-bbctree-83)

The Christmas tree is decorated with lights that are switched on at a seasonal ceremony.[[82]](#cite_note-84) It is usually held twelve days before Christmas Day. The festivity is open to the public and attracts a large number of people.[[83]](#cite_note-85) The switch-on is usually followed by several nights of [Christmas carol](/wiki/Christmas_carol "Christmas carol") singing and other performances and events.[[84]](#cite_note-86) On the [twelfth night](/wiki/Twelfth_night "Twelfth night") of Christmas, the tree is taken down for recycling. Westminster City Council threatened to abandon the event to save £5,000 in 1980 but the decision was reversed.[[81]](#cite_note-bbctree-83)

The tree is selected by the Head Forester from Oslo's [municipal forest](/wiki/Municipal_forest "Municipal forest") and shipped across the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the [Port of Felixstowe](/wiki/Port_of_Felixstowe "Port of Felixstowe"), then by road to Trafalgar Square. The first tree was 48 feet (15 m) tall, but more recently has been around 75 feet (23 m). In 1987 several protesters chained themselves to the tree.[[81]](#cite_note-bbctree-83) In 1990 a man sawed into the tree with a chainsaw a few hours before a New Year's Eve party was scheduled to take place. He was arrested and the tree was repaired by [tree surgeons](/wiki/Arborist "Arborist") who removed gouged sections from the trunk while the tree was suspended from a crane.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

### Political demonstrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Political demonstrations")]

[![A demonstration in Trafalgar Square](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Rally_at_Trafalgar_Square%2C_part_of_the_Mud_March.jpg/250px-Rally_at_Trafalgar_Square%2C_part_of_the_Mud_March.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rally_at_Trafalgar_Square,_part_of_the_Mud_March.jpg)

A demonstration in Trafalgar Square

The square has become a social and political focus for visitors and Londoners, developing over its history from "an [esplanade](/wiki/Esplanade "Esplanade") peopled with figures of national heroes, into the country's foremost *place politique*", as historian Rodney Mace has written. Since its construction, it has been a venue for political demonstrations.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008935-52) The great [Chartist](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism") rally in 1848, a campaign for social reform by the working class began in the square.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008935-52) A ban on political rallies remained in effect until the 1880s, when the emerging [Labour movement](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement"), particularly the [Social Democratic Federation](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Federation "Social Democratic Federation"), began holding protests. On 8 February 1886 (also known as "Black Monday"), protesters rallied against unemployment leading to a riot in [Pall Mall](/wiki/Pall_Mall,_London "Pall Mall, London"). A larger riot, "[Bloody Sunday](/wiki/Bloody_Sunday_(1887) "Bloody Sunday (1887)")", occurred in the square on 13 November 1887.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrick199447-88)

The [Campaign for Nuclear Disarmament](/wiki/Campaign_for_Nuclear_Disarmament "Campaign for Nuclear Disarmament")'s first [Aldermaston March](/wiki/Aldermaston_Marches "Aldermaston Marches"), protesting against the [Atomic Weapons Establishment](/wiki/Atomic_Weapons_Establishment "Atomic Weapons Establishment") (AWE), began in the square in 1958.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008935-52) One of the first significant demonstrations of the modern era was held in the square on 19 September 1961 by the [Committee of 100](/wiki/Committee_of_100_(United_Kingdom) "Committee of 100 (United Kingdom)"), which included the philosopher [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell"). The protesters rallied for peace and against war and [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon"). In March 1968 a crowd of 10,000 demonstrated against American involvement in the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") before marching to the [American Embassy](/wiki/Embassy_of_the_United_States,_London "Embassy of the United States, London") in [Grosvenor Square](/wiki/Grosvenor_Square "Grosvenor Square").[[87]](#cite_note-89)

[![Protests](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/DemonstrationAgainstAntiTerrorismLawLondon23Jan.jpg/250px-DemonstrationAgainstAntiTerrorismLawLondon23Jan.jpg)](/wiki/File:DemonstrationAgainstAntiTerrorismLawLondon23Jan.jpg)

Protesting against harassment of photographers under anti-terrorism law, 23 January 2010

Throughout the 1980s a continuous anti-[apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") protest was held outside South Africa House. In 1990 the [Poll Tax Riots](/wiki/Poll_Tax_Riots "Poll Tax Riots") began by a demonstration attended by 200,000 people and ultimately caused rioting in the surrounding area.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinreb_et_al.2008935-52) More recently there have been anti-war demonstrations opposing the [War in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)") and the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War").[[88]](#cite_note-90) A large vigil was held shortly after the [terrorist bombings in London](/wiki/7_July_2005_London_bombings "7 July 2005 London bombings") on Thursday, 7 July 2005.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

In December 2009 participants from the Camp for Climate Action occupied the square for the two weeks during which the United Nations Conference on Climate Change took place in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen").[[90]](#cite_note-92) It was billed as a UK base for direct action on climate change and saw various actions and protests stem from the occupation.[[91]](#cite_note-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94)[[93]](#cite_note-95)

In March 2011 the square was occupied by a crowd protesting against the government budget and proposed budget cuts. During the night the situation turned violent as the escalation by riot police and protesters damaged portions of the square.[[94]](#cite_note-96) In November 2015 a vigil against the [terrorist attacks in Paris](/wiki/November_2015_Paris_attacks "November 2015 Paris attacks") was held. Crowds sang the French [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem"), [La Marseillaise](/wiki/La_Marseillaise "La Marseillaise"), and held banners in support of the city and country.[[95]](#cite_note-97)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/UK-2014-London-Statue_of_Charles_James_Napier.jpg/250px-UK-2014-London-Statue_of_Charles_James_Napier.jpg)](/wiki/File:UK-2014-London-Statue_of_Charles_James_Napier.jpg)

The statue of Charles James Napier in Trafalgar Square, London

The [Sea Cadet Corps](/wiki/Sea_Cadet_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "Sea Cadet Corps (United Kingdom)") hold a yearly Battle of Trafalgar victory parade, on the anniversary of the Battle (21 October) running the north of [Whitehall](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall"), from [Horse Guard's Parade](/wiki/Horse_Guard%27s_Parade "Horse Guard's Parade") to Nelson's Column.[[96]](#cite_note-98) The [Royal British Legion](/wiki/Royal_British_Legion "Royal British Legion") holds a Silence in the Square event on [Armistice Day](/wiki/Armistice_Day "Armistice Day"), 11 November, in remembrance of those who died in war. The event includes music and poetry readings, culminating in a [bugler](/wiki/Bugler "Bugler") playing the [Last Post](/wiki/Last_Post "Last Post") and a [two-minute silence](/wiki/Two-minute_silence "Two-minute silence") at 11 am.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

In February 2019 hundreds of students participated in a protest against climate change as a part of the [School strike for Climate](/wiki/School_strike_for_climate "School strike for climate") campaign. The protest started in the nearby [Parliament Square](/wiki/Parliament_Square "Parliament Square"), and as the day went on, the demonstrators moved towards Trafalgar Square.[[98]](#cite_note-100)

In July 2020 two members of the protest group [Animal Rebellion](/wiki/Animal_Rebellion "Animal Rebellion") were arrested on suspicion for criminal damage after releasing red [dye](/wiki/Dye "Dye") into the fountains.[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102)

In September 2020 anti-lockdown protests opposed to the imposition of regulations relating to the coronavirus outbreak took place in the square.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

A police observation box has been in the Square since 1919, originally a wooden freestanding unit, it was replaced by hollowing out a lampstand at the southeastern corner of the Square into a permanent structure in 1928, but decommissioned in the 1970s.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

In 2026, protests organised by The Palestine Coalition in Central London, particularly in Trafalgar Square and [Russell Square](/wiki/Russell_Square "Russell Square"), garnered media attention when several pro-Palestine protesters were arrested by the [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") for chanting '[globalise the intifada](/wiki/Globalize_the_intifada "Globalize the intifada").' [[103]](#cite_note-105)[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107) [Met Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") had set specific conditions for Palestine-related demonstrations in the square and throughout [central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London") in part due to the 'changed context' of such phrases following the [Bondi Beach attack](/wiki/Bondi_Beach_attack "Bondi Beach attack") in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia").[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109)

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sport")]

In the 21st century Trafalgar Square has been the location for several sporting events and [victory parades](/wiki/Victory_parade "Victory parade"). In June 2002, 12,000 people gathered to watch [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team")'s [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") quarter-final against [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team") on large video screens which had been erected for the occasion.[[108]](#cite_note-110) The square was used by [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team") on 9 December 2003 to celebrate their victory in the [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2003_Rugby_World_Cup "2003 Rugby World Cup"),[[109]](#cite_note-111) and on 13 September 2005 for [England](/wiki/England_national_cricket_team "England national cricket team")'s victory in [the Ashes](/wiki/2005_Ashes "2005 Ashes") series.[[110]](#cite_note-112)

On 6 July 2005 Trafalgar Square hosted the official watch party for London's bid to host the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") at the [117th IOC Session](/wiki/117th_IOC_Session "117th IOC Session") in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), hosted by [Katy Hill](/wiki/Katy_Hill "Katy Hill") and [Margherita Taylor](/wiki/Margherita_Taylor "Margherita Taylor").[[111]](#cite_note-113) A countdown clock was erected in March 2011, although engineering and weather-related faults caused it to stop a day later.[[112]](#cite_note-114) In 2007 it hosted the opening ceremonies of the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France")[[113]](#cite_note-115) and was part of the course for subsequent races.[[114]](#cite_note-116)

### Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Other uses")]

[![Trafalgar Square temporarily grassed over](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Trafalgar_Square_Grass_-_May_2007.jpg/250px-Trafalgar_Square_Grass_-_May_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_Square_Grass_-_May_2007.jpg)

Trafalgar Square temporarily grassed over in May 2007

As an archetypal London location, Trafalgar Square featured in film and television productions during the [Swinging London](/wiki/Swinging_London "Swinging London") era of the late 1960s, including *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(TV_series) "The Avengers (TV series)")*,[[115]](#cite_note-117) *[Casino Royale](/wiki/Casino_Royale_(1967_film) "Casino Royale (1967 film)")*,[[116]](#cite_note-118) *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")*[[117]](#cite_note-119) and *[The Ipcress File](/wiki/The_Ipcress_File_(film) "The Ipcress File (film)")*.[[118]](#cite_note-120) It was used for filming several sketches and a cartoon backdrop in the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") comedy series *[Monty Python's Flying Circus](/wiki/Monty_Python%27s_Flying_Circus "Monty Python's Flying Circus")*.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELarsen2008203-121) In May 2007 the square was grassed over with 2,000 square metres of turf for two days in a campaign by London authorities to promote "green spaces" in the city.[[120]](#cite_note-122)

In July 2011, due to building works in [Leicester Square](/wiki/Leicester_Square "Leicester Square"), the world premiere of the final film in the [*Harry Potter* series](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)"), *[Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 2](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Deathly_Hallows_%E2%80%93_Part_2 "Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 2")*, was held in Trafalgar Square, with a 0.75-mile (1.21 km) red carpet linking the squares. Fans camped in Trafalgar Square for up to three days before the premiere, despite torrential rain. It was the first film premiere ever to be held there.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

The square has seen controversy over [busking](/wiki/Busking "Busking") and [street theatre](/wiki/Street_theatre "Street theatre"), which have attracted complaints over noise and public safety.[[122]](#cite_note-124) In 2012 the [Greater London Authority](/wiki/Greater_London_Authority "Greater London Authority") created a bylaw for regulating busking and associated tourism.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOdih2019346-125)[[124]](#cite_note-126) In 2016 the National Gallery proposed to introduce licensing for such performances.[[125]](#cite_note-127)

## Cultural references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Cultural references")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Trafalgar_Legoland_2003.jpg/250px-Trafalgar_Legoland_2003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_Legoland_2003.jpg)

A model of Trafalgar Square in [Legoland Windsor](/wiki/Legoland_Windsor "Legoland Windsor")

A [Lego](/wiki/Lego "Lego") architecture set based on Trafalgar Square was released in 2019. It contains models of the National Gallery and Nelson's Column alongside miniature lions, fountains and [double-decker buses](/wiki/Double-decker_bus "Double-decker bus").[[126]](#cite_note-128)

Trafalgar Square is one of the squares on the standard British [Monopoly Board](/wiki/Monopoly_(game) "Monopoly (game)"). It is in the red set alongside the [Strand](/wiki/Strand,_London "Strand, London") and [Fleet Street](/wiki/Fleet_Street "Fleet Street").[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoore2003185-129)

Several scenes in [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s dystopian novel *[Nineteen Eighty-Four](/wiki/Nineteen_Eighty-Four "Nineteen Eighty-Four")* take place in Trafalgar Square, which was renamed "Victory Square" by the story's totalitarian regime and dominated by the giant statue of [Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brother_(Nineteen_Eighty-Four) "Big Brother (Nineteen Eighty-Four)"), which replaced Nelson.[[128]](#cite_note-130)

## Other Trafalgar Squares

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trafalgar_Square&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Other Trafalgar Squares")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Trafalgar_Square%2C_Sunderland_1.jpg/250px-Trafalgar_Square%2C_Sunderland_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar_Square,_Sunderland_1.jpg)

Trafalgar Square in [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland"): a group of [merchant seamen's](/wiki/Merchant_seamen "Merchant seamen") [almshouses](/wiki/Almshouses "Almshouses") dating from 1840

A Trafalgar Square in [Stepney](/wiki/Stepney "Stepney") is recorded in *[Lockie's Topography of London](/wiki/Lockie%27s_Topography_of_London "Lockie's Topography of London")*, published in 1810.[[129]](#cite_note-131)

Trafalgar Square in [Scarborough, North Yorkshire](/wiki/Scarborough,_North_Yorkshire "Scarborough, North Yorkshire") gives its name to the Trafalgar Square End at the town's [North Marine Road](/wiki/North_Marine_Road_Ground,_Scarborough "North Marine Road Ground, Scarborough") cricket ground.[[130]](#cite_note-132)

The square known as [Chelsea Square](/wiki/Chelsea_Square "Chelsea Square"), London SW3 was at one time known as Trafalgar Square and predated the one in Westminster.[[131]](#cite_note-133)

[National Heroes Square](/wiki/National_Heroes_Square "National Heroes Square") in [Bridgetown](/wiki/Bridgetown "Bridgetown"), Barbados, was named Trafalgar Square in 1813, before its better-known British namesake. It was renamed in 1999 to commemorate national heroes of Barbados.[[132]](#cite_note-134)

There is a life scale replica of the square in [Bahria Town](/wiki/Bahria_Town "Bahria Town"), [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), Pakistan, which is a tourist attraction and centre for local residents.[[133]](#cite_note-135)
